Welcome to session number three of the Magnetic Email Marketing System in Toolkit. I'm here with Dan Kennedy and in this session we're going to be talking about the seven different types of emails. Now last we talked about seven different types of subject lines. Actually you added a bonus one. Yes I did. So eight types of subject lines. Today we're going to talk about seven or maybe even more different types of emails you should be sending as well as go over some automated follow-up campaigns that you can model. And of course we'll be showing you lots of examples as well. By the way all of the examples that we're showing you during this part of it is in the toolkit and then a whole bunch more. These aren't all the examples. We would be here all day if we showed you all the examples as much as Dan and I would enjoy that. We will not be here all day. We will not be here all day. Let's talk about when somebody gets a lead. Now this is not just with email but really with any type of business. So somebody opts in and then they really what do they do. Most people don't do anything. Well actually what most people do and what most good sized companies do is they dump everybody into one big general database and everybody gets the same email every day, every other day, every week. And almost regardless of what any of those people do or don't do they just stay in that database and they continue to get the same stuff. So there's no segmentation. There's often no lead generation out of the emails. Well talk about that because I don't think people understand that. Well so hardly any business is one dimensional. Hardly any business sells one thing. So and even if you did there are different reasons that different people have for buying the same thing at different times. So lead generation is about giving people an opportunity to step forward and identify a particular interest to which you now can particularly address. So if you're a chiropractor you have people in your list who have back pain. They have chronic headaches and migraines. You have people in your list who have knee pain, joint pain. You have people in your list who can't sleep. You have people, etc., etc. If you're a financial advisor you have people who are profoundly concerned with creating a safe income that they're not going to outlive. You have other people who have far, they have enough money stacked up that at least in their own minds they are not going to outlive that money but they are concerned with what happens to it after they leave the earthly plane and can they protect it from their near-dwell son and his scheming wife and get it to the grandkids. Somebody else is deeply concerned with perpetuating their charitable giving, etc. So this list, this audience, this population of customers who can buy or could refer, could tell somebody else about you, can be made to respond to lead generation magnet offers extended into your own list. This one this week, a different one next week, a different one next month. You do the same thing by the way in paper and ink newsletters if you're smart. You give people an opportunity to raise their hand and respond to this thing or that thing. You even see, I mean we even do it, not a lot, but we do it in GKIC where there is purely generation offers in email or print media and you have to raise your hand and say, yeah I'm interested in that. Give me the information about that rather than pushing all the information out at everybody. So it's also a way to extend the life of the list looping all the way back to open rates because if you give people opportunity to ask for what interests them and not push things that don't interest them on everybody, you will extend the lifespan that they're willing to stay actively reading you. Right, and I don't want to really get into the technology and we were actually going to talk about list segmentation later. Damn sure, I don't want to get into the technology. But there's, for example, like Infusionsoft or even Aweber and a lot of them now, there's things that you can do based upon what a person clicks on in the email. So they can be segmented into another list so there doesn't necessarily even have to be an offer per se. If they click on this, that shows interest, then they're put into a separate campaign. What came to mind when you were talking about is I received these emails from this company called L&L Publishing, which is a magician's publishing thing. They sell a lot of DVDs and things like that. Now their emails are terrible, but the point is I like it when they send me an email about the newest mentalism thing. I'm interested in that. I want to read that, right? I don't like it when they send me an email about how to do the next cart slice. Right, I have no, and it kind of annoys me. It's the same company, same emails, but the difference is, and all they needed to do was like, look at this guy, he's bought nothing but mentalism DVDs. Why are we sending him that? So you don't have to get super sophisticated with this. No, it wouldn't hurt you to get super sophisticated. And it's easier than ever with the technology. But that, I mean, that's a good first step. Is by this person's clicks, by this person's purchases, by this person's hands raised to different LGMs, different lead generation magnets, they are telling us what they want to hear more about and what they want to hear little or nothing about. So when you don't take advantage of that capability of this, right? You really are doing broadcasting when you don't have to be in a broadcast media. It's foolish. Right, absolutely. Another mistake that most folks make is that there is no follow up, right? I mean, I've signed up for stuff that I've actually wanted to get information on, and maybe I get one email and that's it. And I talk about the mindset of the business owner who thinks, well, they got my best thing, therefore I don't need to follow up anymore. I tend to build very deep and long sequences about a particular thing. Sometimes there's a legitimate limited quantity and then it stops more because we sold it all than for any other practical reason. Now, most people, and again, particularly with email, there's no financial justification for it. So it's just stupidity or sloth. One way or the other. Most people stop talking to someone who has expressed interest in X way, way, way too soon. They leave 10, 20, 30% of their potential productivity. I mean, there's a lot of categories of goods and services where a significant percentage of the buyers are extremely slow about getting to point of purchase. In some cases, these are things people buy that they really don't want to buy, but they know they have to. State planning that you mentioned earlier is a perfect example from the time that I started thinking about buying it to the time that I bought it seven years elapsed. Now, had somebody who communicated with me and I communicated with them in that first year had follow-up that long, they would have got me. Since none had, they all started over at zero at the moment that I really got serious. But there's a lot of categories like this. And with good follow-up, you may be able to shorten that time, but unless you're good at quit and retire in four years, why wouldn't you want the person who is going to take six years to gestate, particularly if you can continue to communicate with them by email? For free. Right? Why? Yeah, why? But well, I think a lot of people because they don't know what to do, how to write it. So therefore, we've helped you out shockingly, not shockingly. In the toolkit, there's a whole bunch of templates. So can we just look at some of the templates? They're going to be probably tough to read on your screen, but really the most important thing that I want to go over here is the psychology of the template and the point of it. All right, so this is after someone, again, this is just one of the templates. This is after someone requests a lead generation magnet. You want to obviously confirm their request, especially if it's not coming immediately. But also, even if it is coming immediately, if you don't have, a lot of people say, well, no, I'm driving them directly to the download page to pick it up. Well, you're assuming that the person is paying attention and that they're going to the download page and that they understood how to download it. So if you follow up with email, then you get another shot at them getting this piece of information that you need them to get. So this first email should accomplish the following things. You're going to want to write this down. If you've got the program, obviously you can just highlight it in the transcript. First is give them immediate feedback that whatever they requested was successful. The second thing is congratulate them on making a really good decision. Why would we do that? So this is the ancient congratulations on being so smart that you bought X. And oh, by the way, that means you'll probably want Y, too. I mean, it sets up the next sale. Look, it is about relationship building. And so people want recognition, appreciation, respect. It's one of the drivers of human behavior. It's a driver of consumer behavior. Getting the opposite of it definitely drives consumers away. There's no doubt about that. And so when you have an opportunity to stroke the ego, you want to do it. Sure. I mean, it's just that simple. Absolutely. So the next thing would be to, and this is to provide contact info if they have any questions. And by the way, not just an email address. I think this is a big, big mistake. A lot of the online guys make. A lot of the info marketers make. But it's really easy today to even outsource this. Giving people another option to contact you other than email is like mission critical, isn't it? Well, you can probably operate without it. You may be in a business that makes you feel compelled to hide and be completely inaccessible. That's possible. I don't recommend being in those businesses, but there are people who are in those businesses. But look, the less availability to helpful humans there is, the more valuable and appealing becomes availability to helpful humans. And in a great many businesses, there are what I call pat on the head questions. They occur before a purchase. They occur after a purchase. They occur between first purchase and second purchase. They perhaps affect a return for a refund. And none of these tend to be difficult or complex or require a great salesperson to your outsourcing point in order to deal with them. They really do require just a reassuring pat on the head. And not having that available, a FAQ page at the website or a FAQ checklist and then we email you the answer is not the same. And there will undoubtedly be a day. We just this month or last month clicked over the there's now more millennials than there are boomers alive. Not more than boomers plus seniors, but boomers, millennials. So there will be a day where everybody will be conditioned to accept no human access and everybody will default to it. So there'll be no competitive disadvantage. Much like everybody now accepts pump your own gas. For fairly long period of time in the transition, people did not accept it. It was a competitive advantage if you still had a guy who would pump the gas. But that's dead. This will be dead too. But not anytime soon. And if you pay attention to advertising, there are companies who are focusing on it. Banks that you can call and actually speak to a human are capitalizing on it. So I think that when you can, if you can, you don't want to compel people to communicate in an impersonal, distanced manner. So that's the third thing. Again, this is all this being accomplished, by the way, in the first follow up email. That's why the templates that you're receiving or that you're going to receive when you invest in the program are so important because all of this psychology is built in. The fourth thing is to get them excited about the thing arriving or resell them on reading or consuming or running out of the mailbox. Yeah, go ahead. Yeah, it doesn't matter whether they're getting it for free or they paid for it. It doesn't really make any difference. If they're getting it for free, we are undoubtedly giving it to them for free to get them now to take the next step and move forward to purchase. So if gardeners get somebody to go, digitally download the book on why you're not getting a good night's sleep, that has accomplished damn little. If we can't get them now to read the book and discover that they need a new mattress. So the assumption would be, well, they went, they downloaded it, clearly they're going to read it. No, bad assumption. All sorts of impulses die on the vine. How many times have you put on your to-do list for the weekend to clean the garage and not go on and clean the garage? The garage was there. The note on the list was there. The original impulse was there, but the garage isn't clean. So initial impulses die on the vine all the time. Purchased products for large dollars, set on a shelf with shrink wrap still on them. Free samples go without ever being consumed, et cetera, et cetera. So it is a very important purpose to sustain and reignite the desire now to somehow consume the thing that they have acquired. So her mind's been on some other things. So while I sent... No, you've got to have a complete campaign, a complete system here. Sure. And that's what we've given you. I'm not going to go through the other ones. They're all in here. This is a four-step follow-up campaign. Now, people would say, well, Dan, why is magnetic marketing three steps? Why is this four steps? Why? Because you've got to... Yeah, well, you've got to teach something. You've got to teach something. Yeah. And this is something somebody can implement. Yeah. If you tell them they're going to put 29 steps in. The original use of a three-step sequence in magnetic marketing came from the collection industry. The pretty standard approach to dunning letters that existed for decades of first notice, second notice, final notice. So it was a pure copycat of something that worked, a movement of something that worked. There's no magic in it being three or four or 12 or 16 in a reality. In most cases, there should be a relatively short number of follow-up steps intensely. And then a maintenance level like forever. And then moving them back into an intense sequence by one mechanism or another. But you've got to teach something. And also, there is the issue of something that if you were fairly grading against best on a one to 10, there's something to be said for a six implemented versus a nine never done. Right. That was beautiful. Both magnetic marketing and now magnetic email marketing, I would not make a claim for them that on a one to 10, they are the be all end all best thing that could be done. I mean, when I create a multimedia follow-up campaign alone for a client that includes email, they're spending $75,000, $80,000, $85,000 just to have me write it, let alone implement it. And it is definitely more complicated than this. However, it embodies most of the same psychology that is built into these templates. The catalog industry, the smart catalogers are getting good at this. So the orvices of the world are getting very good at this. They're driving from a catalog page or a catalog item to an online video to a dedicated email sequence and follow up about that item. It's often how they are able to sell their pricier things than they have ever been able to sell before. So orvis sells hunting clothes and sporting goods and they also sell a $45,000 African safari. Well, there aren't a lot of people who are going to order that off of a catalog page. But they'll go from the catalog page to watch the video about the safari, which the virtue of video is the lion can move. We don't have to show them a still picture of a lion. We actually have a lion moving. And we can do a dedicated email follow up. And by the time we drive that person to a sales rep, we're not starting with, well, what's a safari? And so we're using the sales person productively. And that's exactly what we did for this particular launch of this product. All right. We're getting a bunch of questions here. But first I want to go to talking about, we've talked about the different types of subject lines. We've talked about follow up campaigns that could be put in where giving you a four step follow up campaign, which is literally, literally cut and paste because you don't have to retype it because all the campaigns come on the CD. But let's talk about the different types of emails that people need to send. And I think that one of the big problems that Dan, that people have when they're doing email is they get tied into the same type of email and it gets really boring. And you said the number one marketing sin is being boring. So why is it important to have different, not necessarily different writing style, but different types of emails that you send out? Well, A, you may have different purposes at different times. Right. Right. B, to your point, when you are communicating with the same people again and again and again and again, you have to walk a tightrope between moving away from what works to being so predictable and so routine that people's paying of interest to you diminishes. And so mixing up the purposes, the format, the presentation within an email is the same as mixing it up in any other medium. Absolutely. So let's look at seven different types of emails that I personally write a lot. Write to these different styles. And so the first one, I'm going to go over all seven of them and then Dan, you could add in an extra bonus one or two if you'd like. And then what we'll do is we'll give you some quick examples of each. The first is a Q and A email. This is a really neat one where your subscribers are writing you in questions and then you answer the question. Again, the problem with a lot of email communication is it's one way you're pushing it out, you're pushing it out, you're pushing out. That strategy allows you to obviously get them engaged and get more of a conversation with them. Is that important? Sure. I mean, look, the Q and A format works. It has human interest. It has high readership. It dates at least to Anne Landers and Dear Abby and Ask Hallowe's has some of the most red syndicated newspaper columns in the history of newspaper. And so the surrogate question, the question asked, that represents a lot of people. That's right. That's exactly right. And that they might not necessarily be willing to enunciate. And again, it allows you to mix up the look and the format of these things. So then we've got the testimonial email. We're going to go through these quickly. They're all in the toolkit, obviously. The testimony emails where you send a testimonial. Jim Straw, I saw him use this technique. It was beautiful. His subject line was simply, I love getting emails like this. And then it was like, here's why I'm in the business. And it was a testimonial about the great results he got, then with a link to the product. You've got the classified, I call it the classified, it's a very short email, not a long story or anything like that. It's designed to get people to take some action. Like on some link, call a phone number or do something like that. You've got the pure pitching email. Let's just talk about that one. So this is a pure pitch. I mean, you're just selling. A question, I'll ask you the question now, even though it was going to be for later. People always say, well, what should my ratio be between pitching and not pitching? Well, so first of all, there's pitching and then there's pitching. But if you were talking about a pure pitch. Your pure pitch, right. Now, I'm here to sell you something. Here's the story. Here's the demo. Buy it. It can't be every time you come visit. Or even if I like the stuff, I will be disinclined to let you visit. So it can't be 100%. We can definitively say that. We can definitively say it can't be zero. Unless you are sucking in Wall Street money and taking your salary off the top, you cannot afford to go very long at zero. So we're somewhere between one and 99 right of the time. Is the ratio, right? I mean, look, I think the ratio is fungible. It's flexible. It's not fixed. There are a lot of variables that affect it, including strength of relationship, relevance of offers, you know, all of that. If you put a gun against my head for a number, I probably put it at the one in five. I put it at one in four. Yeah. Neighborhood. That's exactly right. That's exactly what I did. Yeah. Yeah, absolutely. The next one though is kind of a cool one. I call it the disguise pitch. So this does not look like it's a pitch, but the real purpose of the entire email is a pitch. So for example, if you look at the GKIC Web Deal of the Weekend, a lot of times there'll be a story element in there, but it's really a pitch. The real point of that email is to actually sell something. And then one of my favorites, which is the workshop. So can we just go to the workshop one where we won't go through the other examples? The workshop email is something that I got from you that you do in the newsletter. And you call it going on a goose chase or a rabbit hunt or something like that where you have the person have to go do something. And I use this in email and I want to show one, which was, do you have it up there? Okay, perfect. So first of all, talk about the reasons why this is a good idea to have people do this. Because first of all, a good percentage of the time you want some form of active engagement, not just passive receipt. And so this is a form of active engagement. Second of all, people get a little reward shot from it because they were sent on a mission, they accomplished it. So it is, it's why slot machines don't just pay jackpots. It's why they pay little stuff, even less than you wagered. Or you put in a buck, you got 20 cents on this and a bell goes off anyway and you know all that. Because if it just paid jackpots, that's so infrequent that nobody would play. So people like all sorts of positive reinforcement. And candidly in many cases it confuses activity with accomplishment. And so they get this little endorphin, oh boy, I did something, reaction to it. And you know, you may bring somebody in a door by screaming a negative message at them. But you can rarely keep them in a negative, critical environment unless you have them in prison or you know. So positive reinforcement for customers, followers, audience, whatever is very, very important. And so in this email, by the way, it's a very clever email. Oh, thank you. There is sample emails, they all come from ones that I've written in this case, illustrating each one of these points in your toolkit. But this one, Pretty Girls in Cars, let me just give you what this is. Basically I just want to analyze it a little bit very, very quickly. The first is today, Grasshopper. Now again, that's adding personality into the thing and making it not corporate. The basic idea this was, I wrote a story and I didn't tell them what the lessons in the story were. And then they would go to my blog and the person that came up with the best and the most teachings from the story won. Huge response. I mean, I was shocked. People were come up with lessons that I didn't even know I put in the story. All right. And it was fascinating. And then I gave to the best one I gave like a 15 minute phone consultation or something like that. But it got everyone actively engaged. So again, the technique that I use here, this isn't the only way to do it, but the technique I use here is to tell the story, but not tell them what the lessons were in the story, make them figure it out for themselves and they did and it was fantastic. So the key concept though is to get people to actually take action and do something. And again, there's samples for all of the different types of emails in the toolkit. All right. So we've got a bunch of questions here. Okay. The first question is, if you segment your list, how do you search for other interests of your list to add them to more segments? I'm not sure. Not sure I understand it. So again, going back to the Travis example, well, he had different products that people were interested in. Sometimes, by the way, it's as simple as asking them. So hey, if you're interested in, let's go to my magic example. If you're interested in card tricks, click here. If you're interested in mentalism, click here. So sometimes you can just ask them what they're interested in. But a lot of times you're just sending out a new promotion. So I think a lot of people miss what you said, Dan, which was important. So oftentimes you only send out maybe one or two emails about something telling people, hey, if they're interested to click on the link and opt in. And then if they don't click on the link, they never hear... They never see the rest of it. They never see the rest of the promotion. They never see the rest of it. They never see the rest of it. So that's one, that's a way to do it. Stas Ray says, why is everybody suggesting that one email address is worth $1? Well, not everyone is suggesting that one email address is worth $1. I don't know who everybody is. My opinion depends on your market, your product, and your price. So you shouldn't understand it because it's not true. Right. I mean, look, you want to back, and it's an endlessly changing number, but you want to back from some measurement of total customer value to short-term customer value to transaction value to cost per initial sale to cost per lead. And those numbers will give you a dollar number or nickel number or penny number of what a name is worth. And so you will hear direct marketing industry conversations about a name on my list is worth $10.02 a year. A name on my email list is worth $1.13 a year. You'll hear those conversations. They're useful, but they're not complete. Because obviously, if you bust up the sources, there's going to be variants all the way through to by source. Sometimes you will hear that conversation about what they're doing on the backside of renting and exchanging and all that. It has nothing to do with the core economics of the business, but it's really done a particularly useful note. Yeah. So don't believe whoever told you that. Probably one of those gurus. I would start trying to value not just at having any mail address, but at that turning into some kind of further commitment. So now you collected it somehow and now in follow up they have opted in for this X. They have requested X. Now we can start to assign a monetary value to having them do that. Sure. Absolutely. And the next statement we're going to be talking about cash flow surges, holiday campaigns, how to make money, answering more questions about email.
